skill.name: hook
skill.description: Create, update, configure, and validate Claude Code hooks for project automation and tool-event workflows.
skill.summary: Guides Claude Code hook creation, configuration, validation, and safer command usage.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found these are mostly documentation examples and Claude Code hook capabilities, not hidden malware. The skill should publish with a medium-risk warning because it guides creation of hooks that can execute shell commands, alter settings, persist environment variables, and affect tool permissions.

medium_findings.title: Hook generation can enable arbitrary command execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs creation and registration of Claude Code hook scripts. The reference confirms command hooks execute bash commands and warns that hooks can modify, delete, or access user files. This is a legitimate core feature, but unsafe generated hooks could damage data or run unwanted commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly describe command hook execution and local hook script creation. No malicious command is embedded, so the confidence is high for capability risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Hooks can persist environment variables across commands
medium_findings.description: The reference documents CLAUDE_ENV_FILE and includes an example that writes an API_KEY placeholder into the environment file. This is useful for setup hooks, but it can expose or spread secrets if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment persistence behavior and API key placeholder are explicit. The value is a placeholder, so the risk depends on user-generated hook content.

medium_findings.title: Hooks can alter permission decisions and add model context
medium_findings.description: The documentation explains that PreToolUse and PermissionRequest hooks can allow or modify tool calls, and UserPromptSubmit hooks can add context. These features are legitimate, but broad auto-approval or hidden context injection can weaken user control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented with clear examples, including auto-approval logic. The files do not instruct stealthy abuse, so this is a medium contextual risk.

low_findings.title: Path traversal and hidden-file detections are documentation examples
low_findings.description: The static path findings point to example transcript paths and safety guidance. They do not show active file traversal logic or code that reads hidden files for exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are examples of hook input payloads or defensive recommendations. No active traversal or sensitive file read implementation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography and reconnaissance detections are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and reconnaissance hits are caused by ordinary hook documentation, debug commands, event names, or text examples. No evidence found of hashing misuse, network scanning, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation headings and troubleshooting guidance, not cryptographic code or reconnaissance logic. Static pattern matching appears to have overmatched common words and command examples.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found in skill instructions
low_findings.description: A prompt example uses role-style wording for an LLM-based hook, but it is framed as hook behavior rather than an instruction to the auditor or marketplace system. No evidence found of text telling reviewers to ignore instructions, skip analysis, or change risk levels.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed prompt is clearly inside a documented hook configuration example. It does not claim authority over this audit or attempt to override marketplace instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command hooks execute shell commands automatically
dangerous_patterns.description: Claude Code hook configurations can register command hooks that run bash commands when events match. Generated hooks must use narrow matchers, reviewed commands, timeouts, and input validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that command hooks execute shell commands and warns about local file access. This is the central security-sensitive pattern in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment persistence can expose secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: SessionStart hooks can write variables into CLAUDE_ENV_FILE for later commands. Persisting API keys or tokens this way can broaden secret exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The persistence mechanism and sample API_KEY export are explicit. The example is not itself a real secret, but the pattern is security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permission hooks can bypass normal approval flow
dangerous_patterns.description: PreToolUse hooks can return allow decisions, and examples show automatic approval for certain reads. This can be safe when narrow, but unsafe if generated with broad matchers or weak validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission behavior is directly documented. The risk depends on generated policy breadth, so confidence is medium-high rather than very high.

content.value_statement: Claude hook setup is powerful but easy to misconfigure. This skill guides hook creation, settings updates, validation, and safer command patterns.
actual_capabilities: Identifies appropriate Claude Code hook events and matchers for a requested workflow.
Creates or updates hook scripts in the project .claude/hooks directory.
Updates .claude/settings.local.json so hook scripts are linked to events.
Adds input validation, error handling, logging, and comments to generated hooks.
Explains command hooks, prompt hooks, hook input payloads, and structured hook output.
Validates hook behavior after generation without creating separate test files.
limitations: Generated hooks can still run unsafe commands if the requested behavior is unsafe.
The skill focuses on Claude Code hooks, not general CI or deployment automation.
Local settings changes may affect every Claude Code session in the project.
Users must review permissions, shell commands, and file access before enabling hooks.

use_cases.title: Add project command validation
use_cases.description: Create a PreToolUse hook that checks Bash commands against project rules before they run.

use_cases.title: Register local workflow hooks
use_cases.description: Link existing hook scripts to Claude Code events through project settings.

use_cases.title: Build safer prompt guards
use_cases.description: Create UserPromptSubmit or prompt-based hooks that detect risky requests before processing continues.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic hook
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple project hook.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code hook named <hook name> that runs on <event>. It should <requirements>. Keep the command safe and explain the settings change.

prompt_templates.title: Update an existing hook
prompt_templates.scenario: Modify a current hook without losing working behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing hook named <hook name>. Preserve current behavior, add <new requirement>, and validate the settings entry after the change.

prompt_templates.title: Add command validation
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a defensive hook for shell command review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PreToolUse hook for Bash that blocks <unsafe command pattern> and returns clear feedback. Include input validation and safe error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design a full hook workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup with event selection and security review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design and implement a Claude Code hook workflow for <goal>. Choose events, matchers, script language, settings entries, validation checks, and a test plan.

output_examples.input: Create a hook that warns when Bash commands use grep instead of rg.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a project hook script, links it to Bash PreToolUse, validates the command input, and explains how blocked commands appear.

output_examples.input: Update my formatting hook so it runs only after file edits.
output_examples.output: The assistant reviews the existing hook configuration, changes the matcher to file-writing tools, preserves script behavior, and reports the final event mapping.

output_examples.input: Add a prompt guard that blocks accidental secret sharing.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a UserPromptSubmit hook that checks for sensitive terms, returns a user-facing reason, and avoids storing the submitted secret.

best_practices: Review every generated command before enabling the hook in project settings.
Use absolute project paths, quote shell variables, and validate all hook input.
Start with narrow matchers and short timeouts, then expand only after testing.
anti_patterns: Do not create hooks that auto-approve broad tool access without a clear policy.
Do not persist API keys, tokens, or secrets through hook environment files.
Do not run destructive file operations from hooks without explicit safeguards.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create and configure Claude Code hooks that run on events such as tool use, prompts, session start, and stop.

faq.question: Can this skill run commands on my machine?
faq.answer: The skill itself is documentation and guidance, but generated hooks can run shell commands when Claude Code triggers them.

faq.question: Where are hook scripts stored?
faq.answer: The skill directs hook scripts to the project .claude/hooks directory and links them from Claude settings.

faq.question: Does it support prompt-based hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. The reference explains prompt hooks for context-aware decisions and command hooks for deterministic local scripts.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing a hook?
faq.answer: Review shell commands, file paths, environment variables, matchers, timeouts, and any behavior that approves or blocks tool use.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared projects?
faq.answer: It can be safe after review, but shared projects should use narrow hooks and avoid local secrets or broad command execution.
